title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Nerves
content: Taste sensation to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is supplied via the chorda tympani branch of the facial (VII) nerve and the glossopharyngeal (IX) nerve to the posterior one-third; the glossopharyngeal nerve also supplies somatic sensation to the posterior one-third of the tongue. The lingual nerve, a branch of the mandibular division of the trigeminal (V) nerve, supplies somatic sensation to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Cavity (Mouth)  -- Nerves
content: The innervation of the tongue is complex. Motor innervation of all intrinsic and extrinsic muscles, except for the palatoglossus comes from the hypoglossal nerve. The palatoglossus receives innervation from the vagus nerve. The anterior and posterior portions of the tongue have different taste and sensory innervation due to their different embryological origins. The anterior two-thirds of the tongue receives taste sensation by a branch of the facial nerve called the chorda tympani nerve whereas the general sensation of the anterior two-third tongue is by the lingual nerve, which is a branch of the mandibular branch of trigeminal. The posterior one-third of the tongue gets both its sensory and taste sensations from the glossopharyngeal nerve. [1] The buccal nerve, also a branch of the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve innervates the cheek.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Nerves
content: Taste to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is achieved through innervation from the chorda tympani nerve , a branch of the facial nerve (CN VII) . [13] General sensation to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is by innervation from the lingual nerve , a branch of the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V3) . [14] The lingual nerve is located deep and medial to the hyoglossus muscle and is associated with the submandibular ganglion.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Nerve -- Structure and Function
content: In the brainstem, the mandibular branch arises from 3 nuclei (mesencephalic, principal sensory, and spinal) which give rise to its large sensory root. The motor nucleus of the trigeminal nerve, located in the tegmentum of the upper area of the pons, gives rise to the motor root of the nerve [4] . The motor root runs along the trigeminal cave and joins the sensory root before leaving the skull through the foramen ovale and enters the infratemporal fossa. The mandibular nerve is the only branch of the trigeminal nerve that contains a motor root.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Nerve -- Nerves
content: The chorda tympani nerve (branch of the facial nerve) joins the lingual nerve below the skull near the lower border of the lateral pterygoid muscle within the pterygomandibular space [3] . The lingual nerve transports special visceral afferent (SVA) fibers from the chorda tympani responsible for taste perception in the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, and general visceral efferent (GVE) fibers responsible for secretory and motor innervation of the sublingual and submandibular glands [3] . These preganglionic fibers synapse in the submandibular ganglion, suspended from the lingual nerve, and the postganglionic parasympathetic fibers enter the glands [5] . The lingual nerve also has anterior, middle, and posterior communicating branches with the hypoglossal nerve [1] .

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Alveolar Nerve -- Introduction
content: The branches of the trigeminal nerve will further divide into smaller nerve branches to optimally provide sensory innervation to their respective territories. For example, the maxillary nerve will branch into the superior alveolar nerves. Another example is the mandibular nerve branching into the inferior alveolar nerve. The inferior alveolar nerve will be responsible for sensory innervation to the cheek, lips, chin, teeth, and gingivae.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pulp (Tooth) -- Nerves
content: There are two main types of nerves found in the pulp: autonomic and afferent fibers. Sympathetic autonomic nerve fibers found in the pulp originate in the superior cervical ganglion, located superior to the carotid artery. The axonal projections of these unmyelinated neurons innervate the smooth muscle cells of arterioles within the pulp to regulate the contractile forces that support blood flow. The other set of nerves, afferent sensory fibers, originate from the maxillary and mandibular branches of the trigeminal nerve. In contrast to the autonomic nerves of the pulp, these afferent sensory neurons are mostly myelinated, save for their free end terminals, and function in thermal and mechanical nociception. The myelinated portion of these fibers branches extensively beneath the cell-rich zone to form a region called the plexus of Raschkow, while the free nerve terminals extend through the cell-free zone to end up near odontoblasts, sometimes projecting out as far as the dentinal tubules. [9] [10]

title: Cranial Nerve Testing -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Cranial Nerve IX
content: The glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) is responsible for motor (SVE) innervation of the stylopharyngeus and the pharyngeal constrictor muscles by the nucleus ambiguus. Inferior salivary nucleus fibers travel with cranial nerve IX to provide general visceral efferent (GVE) innervation to parotid, buccal and labial glands. In contrast, visceral afferents (GVA and SVA) receive sensory information from the carotid body and carotid sinus and taste from the posterior third of the tongue to synapse on the solitary nucleus. [1] Whereas the sensory afferents (GSA) receive information from the mucosa of the tongue, oropharynx, middle ear cavity, and auditory canal. [6]

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Maxillary Nerve -- Nerves -- 2) Pterygopalatine fossa:
content: The pterygopalatine fossa holds the pterygopalatine ganglion, which is the largest of the four parasympathetic ganglia. It suspends near the sphenopalatine foramen, anterior to the pterygoid canal and medial and inferior to the maxillary nerve. It receives vidian nerve superomedially and ganglionic branches from the maxillary nerve superolaterally. The vidian nerve is formed by the junction of the greater petrosal nerve and deep petrosal nerve within the pterygoid canal. [2] The greater petrosal nerve brings parasympathetic fibers from the facial nerve at the level of the geniculate ganglion, and the deep petrosal nerve brings sympathetic fibers from the carotid plexus. Therefore, pterygopalatine ganglion contains postganglionic parasympathetic and sympathetic fibers along with general sensory fibers of the maxillary nerve. These three types of fibers leave the ganglion as orbital, palatine, nasal and pharyngeal branches. Orbital Branches:

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Nerves
content: The various branches of the trigeminal nerve, or cranial nerve V, provide sensory innervation to the lips. The infraorbital branch of the maxillary division of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V2) supplies the upper lip. The mental nerve derived from the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V3) allows for sensation from the lower lip.

title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Tongue Innervation
content: The sensory and special sensory (taste) functions of the tongue are determined by its sections. Sensation in the anterior two-thirds is supplied by the lingual nerve, a branch of the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V, division 3). The glossopharyngeal nerve (cranial nerve IX) provides sensation to the posterior one-third. Taste perception in the anterior two-thirds travels via the chorda tympani, which initially runs with the lingual nerve and later joins the facial nerve (cranial nerve VII). In contrast, taste sensations from the posterior one-third are mediated by the vagus nerve (cranial nerve X).

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Nerves
content: The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) provides motor innervation to all of the intrinsic and extrinsic muscles of the tongue except for the palatoglossus muscle, which is innervated by the vagus nerve (CN X) . [11] [12] It runs superficial to the hyoglossus muscle. Lesions of the hypoglossal nerve cause deviation of the tongue to the ipsilateral (i.e., damaged) side.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Nerves
content: The nerves that innervate teeth originate from the trigeminal nerve, which is the largest cranial nerve. Distal to the trigeminal ganglion, the trigeminal nerve divides into three branches: the ophthalmic nerve, the maxillary nerve, and the mandibular nerve. The maxillary and mandibular nerves give off branches that innervate the teeth in addition to surrounding gingiva, other soft tissues, and muscle. [12]

title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 9 (Glossopharyngeal) -- Nerves
content: The glossopharyngeal nerve has its origin in the medulla oblongata. It exits the skull via the jugular foramen, where the tympanic nerve branches off to give parasympathetic innervation to the parotid gland. After the jugular foramen are the superior and inferior ganglia, which house the cell bodies of the sensory fibers, and then the nerve descends to the neck, where it provides innervation to the stylopharyngeus and sensation to the carotid sinus and body. It terminates in the pharynx between the superior and middle pharyngeal constrictors, splitting into its other branches – lingual, pharyngeal, and tonsillar (see Image. The Cranial Nerves). [2] The branches of the glossopharyngeal nerve are:

title: Cranial Nerve Testing -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Cranial Nerve XII
content: The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) is responsible for the general somatic efferent (GSE) innervation of the intrinsic and extrinsic muscles of the tongue, except the palatoglossus muscle, from the nerve’s synonymous nucleus. [1] This includes the genioglossus, geniohyoid, hyoglossus, and styloglossus muscles. Fibers from the hypoglossal nucleus exit the medulla from the sulcus between the pyramids and the olives as a collection of fibers that coalesce before entering the hypoglossal canal to exit the cranium. [6] Damage to the nucleus or nerve fibers results in tongue deviation toward the side of the lesion, as the ipsilateral genioglossus muscle becomes weak or flaccid, reducing its ability to protrude the tongue.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Nerve -- Introduction
content: The lingual nerve is a sensory nerve that arises from the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V). The lingual nerve is often in a common stem with the inferior alveolar nerve after the mandibular division enters the infratemporal fossa through the foramen ovale [1] . The lingual nerve separates from the inferior alveolar nerve and then descends anteriorly into the oral cavity. It travels adjacent to the medial surface of the mandibular ramus in the third molar region. As it does so, it innervates the mucous membrane of the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, the floor of the oral cavity, and the adjacent lingual gingiva [2] . Understanding the anatomy of the lingual nerve is crucial for performing safe surgical procedures in its area of distribution, mainly when extracting impacted mandibular third molars, as lingual nerve injury is one of the most serious complications of this procedure [1] .

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Nerve -- Surgical Considerations
content: Injury to the lingual nerve most often results in temporary symptoms, including hyperaesthesia (increased sensitivity), anesthesia (complete loss of feeling),  hypoaesthesia (diminished sensitivity), and dysaesthesia (painful sensation) in the anterior two-thirds of the tongue [7] . Other complications from lingual injury include mechanical (due to biting) and thermal trauma if the tongue and lip are affected by paraesthesia, speaking difficulty, problems with tooth brushing, and sleep difficulty when there is allodynia [14] . Reports indicate that the nerve typically repairs itself within 6 months of damage [15] . If the nerve is injured during dental work and does not repair itself, treatment can be given, including physiotherapy, pharmacotherapy, and stellate ganglion block [16] . It can sometimes be repaired with a direct epineural repair or indirect (graft) neurorrhaphy, most commonly using the sural nerve [4] .

title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques and is positioned within the larger group of Clinical actions A‑G. It encompasses arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints, multiple forms of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy, and cholangiography), and cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region and suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression. Excision also covers orbital exploration by a transcranial approach, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, foraminotomy, partial facetectomy, and disc excision, as well as nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision. Parathyroid surgery falls under this umbrella, including standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and combined mediastinal‑exploration procedures. In addition to these methods, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and maintains a specific relationship with plastic procedures on the nose. Certain extensive spinal procedures—such as multisegment thoracic laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. Together, these relationships define Excision as a versatile operative concept that integrates numerous specialized techniques while delineating its scope relative to broader clinical actions and excluded methods.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of organ‑specific and region‑specific procedures while explicitly excluding certain extensive spinal decompressions. Within this category, adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, and sometimes accompanied by removal of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—uses an excision method. Cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, either with cholangiography or with choledochoenterostomy, is also classified as an excision technique. Exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region falls under the same umbrella. In the spine, excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated intervertebral disc when performed at a single interspace in the cervical or lumbar spine. By contrast, more extensive laminectomy procedures that decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina over more than two vertebral segments—whether cervical, lumbar, or thoracic—are specifically listed as excluded methods for excision. Other procedures grouped under excision include nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, and parathyroidectomy that involves mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach. Excision is positioned within the higher‑level grouping “Clinical actions A‑G,” indicating that it is one of the clinical actions encompassed by that classification. Additionally, excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose and is identified as a method within the broader set of “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” reflecting its relevance to reconstructive and therapeutic interventions in those anatomical regions.

title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
content: Exploration procedure is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent grouping “Clinical actions A – G” and comprises a wide array of specific operative techniques. It includes adrenalectomy, partial or complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct and cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or removal of calculi, which may be accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; exploration of the orbit by a transcranial approach with lesion removal; numerous laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy or discectomy—ranging from 1‑2 vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis) and thoracic spine to more than two segments in the cervical and thoracic spine; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of a herniated intervertebral disc in single cervical or lumbar interspaces; nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct; and parathyroidectomy or exploration of the parathyroids, including variants with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical, sternal‑split or transthoracic approaches. Some of these methods are listed as possibly included within the exploration category, reflecting flexibility in how they may be combined or selected. Together, these procedures illustrate the diverse yet interrelated nature of exploratory surgeries used for diagnostic assessment and therapeutic intervention across multiple organ systems.

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2167
content: Excision is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent group “Clinical actions A‑G.” It includes a wide range of operative methods such as partial or complete adrenalectomy (with or without biopsy) performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, both with and without cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, optionally combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; transcranial exploration of the orbit with lesion removal; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and disc excision in a single lumbar interspace; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration; radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical or transthoracic approaches. The Excision category explicitly excludes certain laminectomy procedures: lumbar laminectomy with exploration and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina that involves more than two vertebral segments, and cervical laminectomy with similar exploration that does not include facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. In addition to these operative techniques, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is related to plastic procedures on the nose. This integrated framework illustrates how Excision groups diverse anatomical surgeries while defining specific procedural exclusions and connections to related therapeutic domains.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide range of operative techniques across several organ systems. It encompasses adrenalectomy procedures—both partial and complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, including cases with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, which may be combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Excision also covers exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region, and various laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) techniques for cervical and lumbar nerve‑root decompression that may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is another method classified under Excision, along with multiple forms of parathyroidectomy—standard cervical parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal split, or transthoracic approaches, and parathyroidectomy combined with mediastinal exploration. Excision is further linked to “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx” and is associated with plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its inclusion in the broader grouping “Clinical actions A‑G.” However, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category, notably laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy (e.g., procedures for spinal stenosis involving one or two vertebral segments). This integrated overview delineates the diverse methods that fall under Excision, clarifies which procedures are excluded, and highlights its relevance for procedural classification and patient‑care planning.

Generic pain classifications (including “Pain, not elsewhere classified”) and specific pain entities such as **trigeminal nerve pain** and **facial nerve disorders** (including **facial paralysis** and upper‑facial atrophy) are linked to peripheral nerve pathology.
